description: Unified eval suite: coding, tool-calling (scheduling + memory), and business chat. Chat prompts target voice-to-text inputs, hallucination calibration, diplomatic constraint satisfaction, and RAG-style long-context retrieval. Tool-call prompts cover scheduling semantics and memory tool usage (OpenWebUI/Onyx memory integration).

function.name: create_appointment
function.description: Schedule a new appointment for a client

service_type.description: Service type: swedish_massage, deep_tissue, hot_stone, sports_massage, facial

appointment_date.description: Date of the appointment to cancel in YYYY-MM-DD format

function.name: reschedule_appointment
function.description: Move an existing appointment to a new date and time

original_date.description: Current date of the appointment in YYYY-MM-DD format

new_date.description: New date for the appointment in YYYY-MM-DD format

new_time.description: New start time in HH:MM 24-hour format

function.name: check_availability
function.description: Check whether a specific time slot is available for a new booking

date.description: Date to check in YYYY-MM-DD format

time.description: Start time to check in HH:MM 24-hour format

function.name: get_client_appointments
function.description: Retrieve existing appointments for a specific client

date_from.description: Start of date range in YYYY-MM-DD format (optional, defaults to today)

date_to.description: End of date range in YYYY-MM-DD format (optional)

function.name: create_memory
function.description: Save important information about the user, their preferences, or their business for future reference. Use when the user shares something they will want remembered across future conversations.

content.description: The information to remember, written as a clear self-contained statement

category.description: preference: how the user likes things done; fact: business or operational data; contact: person or vendor info; task: something to follow up on

function.name: search_memory
function.description: Search previously saved memories for relevant information. Use before answering questions that may have been covered in a prior conversation.

query.description: What to look for in saved memories

coding_prompts.prompt: Write a Python function that implements a trie data structure with insert, search, and prefix-match methods. Include type hints and handle edge cases.

Implement an async rate limiter in Python using asyncio that supports: token bucket algorithm, configurable rate and burst size, and a decorator for rate-limiting async functions. Include proper error handling.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Book Sarah Chen for a 60-minute deep tissue massage on March 15th at 2:00 PM.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Baseline: all args provided explicitly. Tests basic tool invocation and argument mapping.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Please book Marcus Webb for tomorrow morning at 9 — something for his sore upper back, half an hour.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Natural language parsing: relative date (tomorrow=2026-03-12), duration (half an hour=30). Service inference from symptom description — deep_tissue or sports_massage are both valid. Judge should accept either.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Schedule a hot rock session for Jennifer Kim, 90 minutes, March 22nd at 1:00 PM.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Service name normalization: 'hot rock session' → hot_stone. All other args explicit.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Please cancel Priya Sharma's appointment on March 18th — she texted saying she'll be traveling for work.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Tool selection: cancel not create. Model must ignore irrelevant narrative detail (travel reason is optional context, not a required arg).

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Move Jennifer Kim's March 22nd appointment to the 29th at the same time — 1:00 PM.
tool_call_prompts.notes: All args present. Must use reschedule_appointment — not cancel+create. Tests whether the model understands the purpose of the reschedule tool vs. decomposing into multiple actions.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Can you reschedule Marcus Webb's appointment to next week?
tool_call_prompts.notes: Missing: which appointment (original_date) and new time. Model must NOT call reschedule_appointment with guessed/fabricated args — must ask for the missing details.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Is there an opening for a 60-minute session this Friday around 3pm?
tool_call_prompts.notes: Natural date resolution required: 'this Friday' = 2026-03-13. No explicit date provided. Tests check_availability selection over create_appointment (no client name given — not a booking request).

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: What does Sarah Chen have booked for the rest of this week?
tool_call_prompts.notes: Tests get_client_appointments. 'Rest of this week' implies date_from=today, date_to=2026-03-15 (Sunday) — judge evaluates whether date range is sensible, but only client_name is required.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Is Jennifer Kim coming in on the 22nd?
tool_call_prompts.notes: Hard semantic trap. 'Is [client] coming in on [date]' = look up existing appointment, NOT check slot availability. check_availability asks whether a slot is free for new bookings — wrong tool here. Tests whether the model understands tool semantics vs. surface pattern matching.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Marcus Webb wants to know if he can get in for a Swedish massage this Saturday at 10am for an hour.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Intent is to check availability, not book. 'Wants to know if he can get in' ≠ confirmed booking. Model must not call create_appointment despite having all booking args. 'This Saturday' = 2026-03-14.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are a scheduling assistant for a wellness center. Today's date is 2026-03-11 (Wednesday). Use the available tools to help with appointment scheduling. If information required to complete a request is missing, ask for it rather than guessing.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: I'd like to book an appointment.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Maximally underspecified booking request. Model must NOT call create_appointment with fabricated args — must ask for client name, service, date, and time.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant for the owner of Serenity Wellness Center. You have access to memory tools to save and retrieve information across conversations. Save important preferences, contacts, and business facts using create_memory when the user shares something worth remembering. Use search_memory when a question may have been covered in a prior conversation.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Hey, can you make a note — I've set up a new vendor for our massage oil and lotion supplies. We're going with Natura Organics going forward. My contact there is Beth Calloway, her email is beth@naturaorganics.com, and they give us net-30 payment terms. I'll need this when we do the next supply order.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Explicit memory save request. Model must call create_memory with content capturing vendor name (Natura Organics), contact name (Beth Calloway), email (beth@naturaorganics.com), and payment terms (net-30). Category should be 'contact' or 'fact'. Failing mode: model acknowledges the information verbally ('Got it, noted!') without calling create_memory — this defeats the purpose of the memory tool.

tool_call_prompts.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant for the owner of Serenity Wellness Center. You have access to memory tools to save and retrieve information across conversations. Save important preferences, contacts, and business facts using create_memory when the user shares something worth remembering. Use search_memory when a question may have been covered in a prior conversation.
tool_call_prompts.user_message: Just so you know — whenever I'm working through financial projections I always want conservative numbers. Assume 10 to 15 percent below the optimistic case. I've made decisions based on best-case scenarios before and it's hurt us, so from now on I want plans built around the conservative end.
tool_call_prompts.notes: Implicit memory save — user states a durable preference without explicitly asking for it to be saved. A capable model recognizes this is a recurring preference worth persisting and calls create_memory (category: preference) before or alongside acknowledging it. A weak model says 'understood' without saving, which means the preference will be lost when the conversation ends. The preference is specific and actionable: use conservative financial projections 10-15% below optimistic case.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content: okay so I need to put together like a promo email for the clients who haven't been in since like last year um — wait actually make it anyone who hasn't booked in the last ninety days that's probably the right cutoff — and I want to offer them something to come back like a discount maybe ten percent or fifteen I keep going back and forth just pick one that sounds good — and it should feel like a genuine hey we miss you kind of thing not desperate you know but also like we do actually want them to come back and fill up April which has some gaps um — keep it short people do not read long emails like I want it short enough that someone actually reads the whole thing not a newsletter — and professional but warm like how I normally talk not corporate — oh and don't mention any specific services just keep it general

chat_prompts.notes: Voice-to-text single-turn. User corrects themselves mid-sentence (last year → 90 days), leaves discount amount ambiguous (pick one), states a contradictory framing (not desperate / but needs to fill April), and gives the word limit as an afterthought. Good response: extracts the actual intent, makes a clear discount choice and notes the assumption, stays under 80 words, sounds warm not corporate, does not mention specific services. Failing modes: ignores the word limit, asks for clarification on the discount instead of deciding, produces a corporate-sounding newsletter, blends both discount amounts awkwardly.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content: hey quick question I'm putting together our Q2 board summary and I need a few numbers — what's our current client acquisition cost, what's our net promoter score, and what percentage of our revenue is coming from memberships versus one-off visits? just ballpark is fine if you don't have exact numbers

chat_prompts.notes: Grounding / calibration test. The model has no access to Serenity's actual business data — client acquisition cost, NPS, and membership revenue split are not in the conversation. The phrase 'just ballpark is fine' is a deliberate trigger: poorly-calibrated models present invented figures as if they are the business's own metrics. Good response: clearly distinguishes between 'your specific numbers' (unavailable) and 'industry context' (acceptable as clearly-framed reference), and tells the owner where to find each metric. CRITICAL DISTINCTION — acceptable: 'I don't have your data; typical wellness center CAC runs $X-Y'; failing: 'Your CAC is around $X' or 'You probably have an NPS of around Y' (presenting unknowns as the specific business's metrics). The judge should reward explicit acknowledgment that these are not the business's own figures, and penalize any response that blurs that line.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content:
I need to think through a client situation. Here's the email we received:

"Subject: Really disappointed — cancellation charge

Hi, I've been coming to Serenity for almost three years and have never had an issue. Last Thursday I had to cancel my 2pm appointment because my daughter had a sudden asthma attack and I had to rush her to urgent care. I called as soon as we arrived at the clinic — about 2.5 hours before my appointment. I understand you have a policy, but this was a genuine medical emergency and I'm really upset that my card was charged $52.50 without any discussion. I spend over $200 at your studio every month and I would appreciate either a refund or a credit toward my next visit. If this isn't resolved I'm going to have to reconsider my membership and leave a review reflecting how this was handled.
— Margaret H."

Walk me through how to think about this. What are the competing considerations here?

OK, we're not issuing the refund — the precedent risk is too high and the policy exists for a reason. But I don't want to lose Margaret and I definitely don't want a one-star review from a three-year client. Write the response email. It needs to feel genuinely human — not a form letter. She should feel heard and respected. Don't be sycophantic, don't cite the policy as justification, and don't offer a refund or monetary credit. But find something real to offer her.

chat_prompts.notes: 2-turn diplomatic constraint satisfaction. Turn 1 tests analytical framing of competing priorities. Turn 2 tests hard constraint satisfaction in professional writing: (1) no refund or monetary credit — hard constraint, (2) warm and genuine — not a form letter, (3) not defensive or policy-citing, (4) not sycophantic, (5) offers something real (e.g., complimentary add-on at next visit, a personal note from the owner, priority rebooking). Failing modes: offers a refund or credit despite Turn 2 instruction, produces a corporate form response, opens with sycophantic praise of the client, lectures Margaret about the policy, or offers nothing concrete.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content: Draft a brief internal memo to all staff announcing that we're raising deep tissue and hot stone service prices by ten dollars across all session lengths, effective April 1st. The reason is increased supply costs and a therapist compensation adjustment. Keep it informational and professional — staff-facing only, not client-facing.

actually wait hold on um forget that pricing memo — the whole price increase is getting pushed to Q2 we decided to wait so just scrap that entirely — what I actually need right now is a staff meeting reminder for Monday um it's at nine am in the break room attendance is required not optional — main topics are Q1 financials review and then the float therapy discussion — for the float therapy part I want the reminder to sound genuinely excited because we are we think this could be a real opportunity for us — keep it short enough to drop in the staff Slack channel people aren't going to read a wall of text — oh and just to be super clear do not include anything about pricing in this that's a completely separate thing now okay

chat_prompts.notes: 2-turn voice-to-text pivot. Turn 2 is a VTT message that completely abandons Turn 1 and requests something entirely different. Tests whether the model: (1) fully discards the pricing memo content — zero pricing references allowed, (2) correctly produces a meeting reminder for Monday 9am, break room, required attendance, (3) captures the 'genuinely excited' framing instruction for the float therapy item despite it being buried in rambling VTT, (4) applies a professional memo tone. Failing modes: bleeds pricing content into the reminder, misses the excitement framing for float therapy, treats attendance as optional, or loses track of which task was requested.

chat_prompts.system_prompt:
You are a business advisor for the owner of Serenity Wellness Center. Answer questions accurately using the operations reference below. For specific facts, contacts, and figures, use only what is in the reference — if something is not listed, say so clearly rather than estimating.

[OPERATIONS REFERENCE]

SERENITY WELLNESS CENTER — STAFF OPERATIONS REFERENCE
Updated: January 2026

STAFF DIRECTORY

Dana Wells | Senior LMT | Full-time, Mon–Thu
Specialties: deep tissue, sports massage, prenatal massage. Certifications: LMT, Prenatal Massage Certification (PMC), Myofascial Release Level 2. Primary room: Room 2. Dana mentors new practitioners. Her Tuesday morning slots (9–11am) are reserved for three long-term clients (Elena R., Paul M., and Gwen T.) — do not reschedule without her approval.

Rob Kim | Senior LMT | Full-time, Mon–Fri
Specialties: hot stone, Swedish, reflexology. Certifications: LMT, Hot Stone Therapy Certification, Reflexology Practitioner Certificate. Primary room: Room 1. [CONFIDENTIAL: Rob has given notice and will leave in June 2026 to open his own practice. This is not yet disclosed to staff or clients. Do not reference in any client-facing communications.]

Keiko Tanaka | LMT | Part-time, Tue/Wed/Fri
Specialties: Swedish massage, facial treatments. Certifications: LMT, WA State Esthetics License. Start date: March 10, 2026. During her first 60 days, Keiko may not be booked for deep tissue, sports massage, or hot stone services. Assign to Rooms 5 or 6 when possible.

Marcus Webb | LMT | Part-time, Sat–Sun only
Specialties: sports massage, deep tissue. Certifications: LMT, Certified Sports Massage Therapist (CSMT). Available 9am–5pm weekends only.

Priya Sharma | LMT | Part-time, Mon/Wed/Thu
Specialties: Swedish, hot stone, prenatal massage. Certifications: LMT, Prenatal Massage Certification (PMC).

Lisa Park | Front Desk Manager | Full-time, Mon–Sat
Manages: scheduling, client intake, payment processing, supply ordering, client communications. Vagaro administrator access. Primary contact for all vendor inquiries.

TREATMENT ROOMS
Room 1: Massage (Rob Kim primary)
Room 2: Massage (Dana Wells primary)
Room 3: Massage (overflow/general)
Room 4: Prenatal-equipped (hydraulic table, positioning bolsters) — required for all prenatal services
Room 5: Multi-purpose — currently facial treatments; reserved for float tank conversion if approved
Room 6: Multi-purpose — currently facial treatments; reserved for float tank conversion if approved

HOURS
Monday–Saturday: 9:00 AM–7:00 PM (last appointment slot: 6:00 PM)
Sunday: 10:00 AM–5:00 PM (last appointment slot: 4:00 PM)

SERVICE MENU AND PRICING
Swedish Massage: 60 min $95 / 90 min $135
Deep Tissue Massage: 60 min $105 / 90 min $145
Hot Stone Massage: 60 min $115 / 90 min $155
Sports Massage: 60 min $100 / 90 min $140
Prenatal Massage: 60 min $95 (no 90-min option; Room 4 required)
Facial — Standard: 60 min $85
Facial — Premium (includes microdermabrasion): 75 min $110
Express Recovery Massage: 30 min $55 (returning clients only; new clients require a 5-min intake first)

MEMBERSHIP PROGRAM
Monthly fee: $89/month (auto-renewing)
Benefits: 15% discount on all services, priority booking (24-hour early access before general availability), one guest pass per quarter
Cancellation: 30-day written notice required. No mid-cycle proration.
Active members: 47

CANCELLATION AND NO-SHOW POLICY
Required advance notice: 24 hours for fee-free cancellation
Late cancellation (under 24 hours): 50% of service fee charged to card on file
No-show (no contact before appointment time): 100% of service fee charged
Exceptions:
  — First-time clients: waive late cancellation fee once as a goodwill gesture
  — Medical emergencies: use judgment; document every exception in Vagaro with the reason and name of approving staff member
  — Repeat offenders (3 or more late cancellations within 12 months): require prepayment at booking; no further exceptions

GIFT CARDS
Valid 12 months from date of purchase. Non-refundable. Transferable to another client. Lost cards reissued if original purchaser provides purchase date and amount.

REFERRAL PROGRAM
$15 account credit to referrer when referred client completes their first full-price service. Credits do not expire. Not applicable toward membership fees.

EMERGENCY AND VENDOR CONTACTS
Medical emergency: Call 911 immediately, then notify owner at 206-555-0293.
Building maintenance emergency (after hours): Tom Reeves (building manager), 206-555-0147.
Equipment repair (tables, HVAC, linen infrastructure): Wellness Equipment Services, 206-555-0382. Business hours only — leave voicemail after hours, they respond next business morning.
Linen supplier: Pacific Linen Services, account number PL-4471. Contact: Rodrigo Vega, rodrigo@pacificlinen.com.

FLOAT THERAPY (PROPOSED — NOT YET APPROVED)
Rooms 5 and 6 would convert to float tanks (Zen Float Co. partnership). Price: $95/session, available in 60 or 90 min. Capital investment: $95K. Projected payback: 18–24 months at target utilization. Each session requires a 10-minute orientation by certified staff. Decision pending Q2 2026 planning meeting.

turns.content: Quick question before this morning's bookings — Keiko is on the schedule for later today. She started with us ten days ago. What can and can't she be booked for right now, and which rooms should she be in?

okay also um Lisa called in sick today which is terrible timing because our linen delivery is tomorrow — who do I contact for that and what's our account number so I can handle it directly? Oh and while I have you — a member just texted asking about the premium facial, what does she pay after her discount and how long is that service?

chat_prompts.notes: RAG-proxy retrieval test. System prompt contains a dense operations reference (~900 tokens) simulating an Onyx/OpenWebUI document injection. Tests precise recall of specific facts from different sections of the document under realistic context load. Turn 1 requires: Keiko restriction (no deep tissue, sports, or hot stone during first 60 days) + room assignment (Rooms 5 or 6). Turn 2 voice-to-text requires: linen contact (Rodrigo Vega, rodrigo@pacificlinen.com, account PL-4471) + member premium facial price ($110 × 0.85 = $93.50) + duration (75 min). All answers are in the reference — a model hallucinating or estimating any of these specific details (contact name, email, account number, prices, durations) should score lower. Good response cites specific values without hedging; does not invent or estimate.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content: Create a 75-minute quarterly planning agenda for next Friday. Attendees: me (owner), Dana and Rob (lead therapists), Lisa (front desk manager), and our bookkeeper Marcus. Topics to cover: Q1 financials, summer staffing plan, the float therapy decision, and client retention numbers.

Marcus just told me he can only stay for the first 45 minutes — he has another client. Restructure the agenda so everything he needs to be present for (the financials and anything involving budget decisions) happens in the first 45 minutes. The float therapy discussion and staffing plan can happen after he leaves. Keep the total at 75 minutes.

chat_prompts.notes: 2-turn restructure. Turn 2 tests whether the model genuinely reorders the agenda (financials and budget items first, float therapy and staffing after) rather than just appending a note. Total must still be 75 minutes.

chat_prompts.system_prompt: You are a business advisor helping the owner of Serenity Wellness Center — a mid-size day spa and massage therapy studio — with planning, communications, and business decisions. Be direct, concise, and professional.

turns.content:
Here's where we are as a business — I want you to have the full picture before we start planning.

Serenity Wellness Center opened in March 2021 in the Eastside neighborhood. We have 6 treatment rooms, 8 practitioners (Dana Wells and Rob Kim are our two senior full-time therapists; the other 6 are part-time), and Lisa Park runs our front desk. I own and manage the business.

Revenue: $680K in 2025, up 22% from 2024 ($557K). We've been profitable since month 14. March 2026 is our best month ever — on track for $72K.

Bookings: averaging 1,100 per month for the past 6 months. We're running at 84% capacity. Our no-show rate is 8% (we implemented a deposit requirement in September 2025, down from 19% before that). 71% of clients rebook within 60 days.

Services: Swedish massage, deep tissue, hot stone, prenatal massage, sports massage, and facial treatments. Average ticket is $95.

Q1 2026 challenge: Rob Kim informed us in January that he's leaving in June to open his own practice. He's our highest-rated practitioner (4.9 stars, 340 reviews) and manages roughly 30% of our monthly bookings. We've started recruiting but the local therapist market is tight — we've interviewed 6 candidates and made 2 offers; both declined citing our compensation structure. We currently pay practitioners 42% commission.

Float therapy opportunity: We've been approached by a float tank supplier (Zen Float Co.) about becoming their first wellness center partner in the city. Float therapy (sensory deprivation tanks) commands $75–$95 per session, has very low per-session labor cost once installed, and has strong repeat usage. Installing 2 tanks would require converting 2 treatment rooms and approximately $95K in capital investment. Payback period estimated at 18–24 months at projected utilization.

Staffing plan: To replace Rob and handle float therapy intake (float sessions need a brief orientation), we'd need to hire 2 full-time practitioners. We're considering raising commission to 47% to be more competitive — this reduces our margin per session from 58% to 53% but would put us above market rate.

Key decision for Q2 planning: Do we proceed with float therapy now (while we have capital and momentum) or wait until Rob's replacement is stable? Our accountant Marcus Chen advises waiting; Dana Wells thinks the float opportunity is time-sensitive and we should move.

Please create a focused 60-minute planning agenda for a decision meeting next week. Attendees: me, Dana, Lisa, and Marcus (our bookkeeper/accountant). Rob will not be attending — we haven't announced his departure to the full team yet.

Good. For each agenda item, add 2–3 specific discussion questions that will move us toward a decision. Mark each item as DECISION (needs a clear outcome from this meeting) or UPDATE (informational only).

One more thing — I need to write a short brief for Dana before the meeting. She's passionate about float therapy and will advocate hard for it, but she doesn't have the full financial picture. Write her a 150-word pre-read that gives her the context she needs to have a productive conversation: specifically, what the float therapy investment involves, what our staffing situation looks like right now, and why Marcus is advising us to wait. Use the actual numbers. Don't sugarcoat the staffing risk.

chat_prompts.notes: 3-turn context stress test grounded in the wellness center. Turn 1 is ~650 tokens of business detail. Turn 3 asks for specific numbers and facts from Turn 1: $95K investment, 18–24 month payback, Rob's departure timeline, 2 declined offers at 42% commission, Marcus's advisory position. Models with truncated context will produce vague pre-reads missing the financial specifics and staffing reality.
